President Donald Trump holds his 9th cabinet meeting at the White House.

byIsabelle Hanson

COLUMBUS, Ohio (WSYX)  —Three central Ohio restaurants posted on social media that they'reprotesting the Paris Olympicsand won't be broadcasting the Olympics games.

They cite a scene from the opening ceremony as their reason.

"The portrayal of The Last Supper was handled with an irresponsible level of irreverence, insulting the Christian faith," the post included.



The restaurants sharing the post are TownHall and Mandrake, both located in the Short North and Rebol in Bridge Park.

"Honestly, I think it’s kind of ridiculous," Wade DeWitt said, who lives across the street from TownHall.

Some said the scene in the opening ceremony mocked Leonardo Da Vinci's painting of The Last Supper, which is part of the Christian faith. Others said it portrayed the feast of Dionysus, a Greek god.

The Olympicsposted on "X" Friday, "The interpretation of the Greek god Dionysus makes us aware of the absurdity of violence between human beings."

WSYX

"We’re in a period of time where everyone is compelled to share their thoughts immediately and have a take immediately, even if no one has really asked that of them," said Tony Elder, who works a few storefronts down from TownHall at Torso.

The restaurants' decision received a mixed bag of comments on Instagram. Some in support, including, "Stopping for a bowl today." "Thank you." "Well done."

Others commented, "I'm a Christian, and I'm unfollowing you. It wasn't the Last Supper. This is dumb." "Get a grip and stick to making green goddesses and espresso martinis."

The restaurants' social media post also included, "We unapologetically uphold a standard where all religions, races, sexual orientations, national origins, disabilities, ages, and gender identities are respected. We will not tolerate any form of discrimination or desecration.”

DeWitt said the restaurants' decision feels discriminatory, considering drag queens were featured in the segment they're referencing.

"100 percent, yeah," he said. "It’s like speak what you preach."

"It wasn’t like anything was being titillating or sensational," said Elder. "It was a performance, and there’s always an abundance and an opulence to the opening ceremonies."

The International Olympic Committeeposted an apologyon "X." “In the daily press briefing, the Organising Committee said that there was never any intention to show disrespect towards any religious group or belief.”

The restaurants' limited their comments on the Instagram post about the decision.

ABC6/FOX28 stopped in the restaurant twice and asked to speak with someone about the decision, but the employees said no one was available and wrote down the reporter's phone number. This article will be updated if the reporter hears from anyone.

"I don’t know whose decision it was," said DeWitt. "I don’t know if it was one person or three people. I think it’s a bad PR move, and I won’t be going there anymore."

2025Sinclair, Inc.
title: Columbus area restaurants decide not to show Olympics after opening ceremony controversy
description: Three central Ohio restaurants posted on social media that they're protesting the Paris Olympics and won't be broadcasting the Olympics games.They cite a scene
